questions.text: I can usually tell when someone is holding back what they actually want to say.
I find that the felt weight of another person's hardship is the factor that most reliably corrects my tendency toward self-centered decision-making.
I experience anger as a physical heat that radiates through my body.
I find it natural to tell others what I expect from them.
I find that I'm able to remain present and attentive even in emotionally charged conversations.
I'd rather produce a rough first draft quickly and refine it than aim for thoroughness upfront.
I feel that meaningful things can be accomplished without anyone formally leading.
I believe that the consistency with which I take others' suffering seriously is the best indicator of whether my values are genuine.
I often describe my emotional experiences using superlatives — best, worst, most intense.
I hold the view that orderliness in small things reflects clarity in thinking.
I detect when a group is performing harmony to avoid conflict.
I hold the view that people should communicate their needs clearly rather than expecting others to guess.
I believe that decisiveness is one of my core strengths.
I believe that knowing what I want and going after it is a sign of maturity.
I think through potential problems before they arise so I can prevent them.
I hold the view that objectivity in the face of others' hardship is a form of strength that benefits everyone involved.
I find that shrinking my own wants in favor of others' needs comes naturally.
I tend to spend my weekends doing things for other people rather than pursuing my own hobbies.
